transcript.text: Oh, sorry.

Yeah.

How was your weekend?
Hectic.
Hectic weekend.
I mean, yeah.

So I have plans to spend the weekend by myself in the city because I've been here since Christmas and there was no one day I was with myself, by myself, walking, you know, which is something that I really enjoy doing.
And, uh, but I, uh, my phone died.
Absolutely.
Like it went, you know, when it doesn't die, but it has this black bluish screen and you can do no reset, no nothing.
And so I literally have no Google maps, no Uber, no nothing.
Like, so I ended up buying a new phone the next day and I accompanied Nadia to a four year old birthday party.
So, and then I, someone invited me to.
Super Bowl party in the city on Sunday, which probably wasn't a good idea, but I couldn't go anyways, because I was, I have no, I'm incommunicado.
So, but I don't know.
Well, good you didn't get completely stranded.
I thought that's where you were going to go with that, because, you know, maybe less familiar with the area and so relying on phones.
I know, I know.
I super, I mean, if I'm in the city, I'm not that lost.

I've been here, like, many times, but the coming and going from Oakland Hills, where Marcel and Maria live and all that, I mean, it's like, I'm in despair, because it's not that I can just stop a taxi on the street these days.
So, but anyway, other than that, it was a good weekend.
I'm exhausted anyway, so I wonder where the weekend went.
What about you?
That was pretty good.
I was sick most of the weekend, unfortunately.
Oh, yes.
I'm sorry, I didn't ask.
Yeah, no, I'm, no, all good.
Now, you know, still a little bit like congested, but so much better than I was like Thursday, Friday, last week.
George just told me he also was sick.
Oh, George got sick too.
Oh, no.
Yeah, like he said he was, was his nose running or I don't know what it's, what was it?
Yeah.
You feel better now?
Much better, much better.

Unfortunately, my team got destroyed in the Super Bowl yesterday, so I was not thrilled with that, but it is what it is, but so for this session, I know you had a chance to join the tail end of this and also catching you a little bit later in your process of getting up to speed here.
I know.
So what would be most helpful?

Like I can obviously run through what I would usually run through, but maybe this is already context you've gathered, so are there key things that are just like top of mind for you that you, you know, wish you had more contact?
I may not be able to help there, but happy to make sure this is as useful as possible for you.
Yeah, of course.
Just I don't mind hearing things repetitively because that's the way in which the information sinks in, you know.
So, I mean, I've been hearing many things and somehow understanding things, but I am still in the moment where some things are still isolated, like disconnected.
So, the more I hear and the more it gets said, you're ready to become.
Okay, cool.
Well, we can go with that and feel free to stop me along the way if you have any questions or, you know, this should, again, be helpful for you.
So, as well as like if you've already heard some of this stuff before, I don't want to waste time there.
So, let me jump into a couple things.
So, amazing we have all these logos.
You know, very excited to be able to work with all these amazing brands.
I think I had mentioned this to you previously, just trying to put this away.
Prior to joining GrowthX, I actually was the first customer when I was leading growth at Homebase.

And so I've been able to see a very unique kind of progression of a lot of different sides of things, being a customer, then coming in and working with a lot of our customers and setting the strategy, then doing a quick stop and selling to a lot of our customer, our prospects, and then coming back and doing, you know, a version of what I'm doing now for customers today.

And so the thing that I feel we're getting better at understanding as a team, but we lost a little bit of connection with, and I used to use this opportunity in this session to our people who had just joined to make sure they are thinking about this is why are these people coming to us?
Like,
What is top of mind?
I mean, there's so many different things you can break down, and there's going to be some that are going to be unique to those brands.
What they need to do is they need to drive growth.
They need to drive reputable revenue.
They oftentimes need to diversify where that revenue comes from.
And VC-backed companies, a lot of times, they tend to go this path of, in the early phases, they go after channels like paid search.
So they're finding different keywords that make sense for their business.
They're going to run ads on that on Google and other sources.
And that tends to be, more often than not, what can be thought of like demand capture.
So people are actively searching for something.
You are giving them an ad.
You're saying, hey, we have this.
And then you're trying to convert them into an action, right?
And so when you have all that pressure of being VC-backed and growing repeatedly and fast, it makes sense why a lot of companies go that direction.
The challenge is, though, there's natural headroom there.
It's like, you can only grow so much.
You don't want to be so reliant on a single channel.
So a lot of times why I got hired was because brands got stuck in that pattern and they needed to diversify.
And that could be other paid tactics.
That could be events, organic channels, such as organic search or AI visibility, AEO comes to mind.
Because if you can, they have typically a lot more headroom and if you can make that work, the economics of that is just so favorable.
So there's so many brands that are at different phases, right?
Like, you you talk about the web flows of the world.
They already have tons of organic presence and they've done a lot of different things.
Some of these brands come to us and they have next to no foundation for that.
And we truly work with a lot of different phases, but the commonality between them all is they're coming to us because they need to drive more revenue, conversion, whatever that may be.

Through organic channels and content being a foundational aspect of that and us starting to find different ways where we can more efficiently grow them, grow content and grow those channels by understanding their businesses at deep level.
That is really where we started to get that like early hook and being able to be that partner of, hey, you can't really grow these channels without content.
It's expensive to hire these teams if you take it back to home base where I was leaving growth before.
I inherited a team of freelancers, a couple in-house people.
They spent a whole bunch of money on different things and it was like doing okay.
But it was like clear, like it wasn't consistent growth.
It was more trending down or like up and down and like it's a super consistent pattern for these brands.
And what I had to do was I had to go in, I had to assess like who's good here, who's not like, what are we even doing strategically?
How do
How I review that what we put in terms of content is aligned with that?
How do we get that content live on the website?
And when you consider all those different steps, that can take quite a while to start to get in action.
And there's a lot of these brands suffer from that.
I see.
So let me ask you, is there sort of like a level of maturity that a customer needs to have to be a good customer for us?
It's a good question.
I would definitely encourage you, if you haven't already, to find time at some point to chat with George about the work he's done around identifying our ICP and putting a model against that.
because that's definitely progressed over time, right?
Like, you know, there's there's a few different things, right?
There's how bought in are they on the concept of like needing to invest here is an obvious one, right?
Obviously, obviously, there's some intent at their top.
But there can sometimes be varying levels of like, you know, urgency behind that.
And then there is also how practical are they about how quickly you can grow a channel like this?
If you're going to come to us and you want us to grow your revenue, whatever, like super aggressively in like a month or two, you're probably not investing in the right channel.
You're probably not thinking about this in the right way and you're probably not going to be the most ideal partner for us.
Okay.
There's other things, certainly, like we consider different things of like certain industry patterns we've seen or complexity of that content we would need to create.
Because that can be quite a bit, right?
If you're speaking to an audience that you need legal medical review to make sure we're not saying something in a certain way.
It's not that we haven't done that.
And it's not that that's not possible.
It just adds to the complexity.
So if you have someone who wants like results overnight or is speaking to a super tech.
Technical audience.
It's going to take tons of review.
That's all friction for us to being able to get content out as fast as possible.
And really what we're trying to do, going back to what I was mentioning earlier of building these teams and systems, we're trying to get signal as fast as possible.
If we get faster signal, we learn faster what's working, what's not.
And if we can do that, then we become an effective partner for them.
If we can't do that, there's friction to getting that signal.
It's going to be hard for us to show value, drive the outcomes they want to see.
Okay.
So maybe it's just a detail, but because of all the things that I am trying to, I will try to put together.
I want to do like an, an agentic workflow for contracting.
So we can make contracting easier for our salespeople.
So, because even in the smallest back and forth, or can we accept this governing law, or can we change these in the liability section that if I can take that out.
Out of the time and mental space of account executives, I think it's worth it, especially in the face of where we are, which is a startup, and this will keep on growing.
So I'm trying to build sort of like a workflow.

And when I was reviewing this, I was wondering, I see that we have three types of customers, at least like the, not the name convention, but the classification is like a standard customer, strategic customer.
And I wondered, and then the level of complexity of some customers, which I reviewed in the Notion database.
But I was wondering, what, how do we do when we have a customer, say, I don't know, someone in the, as you mentioned, in the health industry, like it's a regulated industry.
You have a specific regulation that compliantly will be like immersing whatever we deliver.
So I was wondering, how do we deal with that?
Because I.
Do we have a process?
Do we review it with legal counsel?
Yeah, it's a good question.
I mean, that is part of what the strategy sprint is trying to help us do, is to be able to understand their goals, their objectives, the different things that we need to really build the boards.
What is the strategy to get there?
Based off of that strategy, what is it that, like, what do we actually need to do tactically to build towards that?
Like, what kind of, you know, does that content strategy look and feel like aligning with them on that?
And then starting to calibrate and execute against that.
And so, as we do that, that should help us align on what does it actually mean to get to that, like, their bar of what they see as success from, like, content outputs.
And ideally, that is highly aligned.
Thank
And with the strategy, we're also aligning on with them.
And so there's no doubt like common patterns that we've learned from other customers, we can pull in for a complicated, you know, healthcare type situation.
But also there's going to be their own requirements that are going to be a little bit different, you know.
So we need to learn what does that actually look like for them.

And as we do that and we calibrate on that quality and that output of content, we're also should be continually looking at every time we produce something for them, how do we consistently hit that bar and how do we make it more efficient?
And so what that looks like, you know, there, again, there's like common tactics we can bring out there, but, you know, take someone like a Biologica who did just recently churn, unfortunately.
That was like a really interesting situation, though, where they...
Had us write about a variety of different ingredients for this supplement they have, which is the bulletproof thing.
Yeah, it's like targeting pre and post-menopausal phases for women.
But what's unique about that is not only do they have, we have to talk about those ingredients and its impact on that life phase for women in an accurate way.

They also look at it from more like a holistic medicine standpoint that's a little bit unique than what you may see average in the market if you were just to look broadly of like, how does this ingredient, whatever.
So anyways, long way of saying that, like that forced us to build some muscle around like, what does it take to actually do this?
Like, how do you kick off different reviews?
How do you fact check?
Like, how do you have a knowledge base that you can continue to enrich over time we have?
And so it may not be that we solve that, what you're kind of thinking of.
But if we can reduce the time it takes to get to that point and be creative and, you know, continue to think about it systematically, we should be able to do some more efficiently.

That doesn't mean we always can get to that bar and that's sometimes the challenge when you have these really, like, sometimes just like, you know, there's different things they want and it's just like, okay.
Big one.
We can't check all these boxes right now.

So, you know, sometimes there has to be that back and forth of like, what's practical and realistic and then frankly, like, you know, at times where just like based off of industry requirements and things like that, it is hard to get to a level where it's just like, you could, you could ever reasonably, you know, do this systematically using even the best workflows, agentic work.
Yeah, it needs human input.
And even then it's like, even if you did this.
What is even possible there?
Because humans aren't infallible, right?
And so this is a lot of what like Panzer focuses on and helps us figure out.
And so when it comes into those situations where we need to, like, how do we, this is sticky, this is complicated.
How do we figure this out?
Oftentimes that's where we would bring in him.
Okay.
Interesting.

Like, what is our bread and butter?
Or what do we want to ultimately, like, have each engagement look like as much as possible?
In the early days, we kind of did a variety of different things, although it's...
It's skewed, you know, relatively editorial, but we've learned time to time again.

Oh, yeah, of course.

So the key thing to think about here is I wouldn't necessarily say this is, like, source of truth for, like, all our work streams and our deliverables today.
Think of this as this is just trying to communicate a little bit of what's possible.
We have done a variety of different things that we've, like, layered on at different points and taken on some pretty custom engagements.
What we have learned, though, is, like, if people are planning to lean into some of these other things first and foremost, and they only want that, it's maybe not as much of a fit.
The relationship...
The that tend to go the best is when we start here with developing the editorial and content refresh foundation.
And that's why you see so much of what we do is the process and our platform is built around that in a lot of ways.
And one reason for that is there's just so much we can learn from this approach that we can build and compound over time.
Whether that be, you know, ultimately what we're really trying to understand is like, who is their ICP that they really want to prioritize?
Understand their business at a really deep level, how they are uniquely positioned to help them through their pain points and challenges.
What kind of questions do those people have associated to all those pain points and challenges?
And how can we create really amazing, engaging answers and content aligned with that?
And if you do that, well, that should build a lot of brand equity and compounding organic growth that can start to lay.
The foundation where you can do a lot of the other things.
But if you don't do this super well, sometimes it's like you're putting the carp up for the horse and you're jumping in.
Like what we're ultimately trying to do is get as many qualified eyeballs, if you will, people, website as possible.
And then you can start to layer in these different things.
Because if you just jump straight to newsletters, let's say, and you don't have any traffic, what's the point of a newsletter if no one's on your website?
Like you're not going to convert anyone to it because you don't have, so like first you need to like build that foundation and like learn from that.
And then as you do that, there's so many rich learnings you can apply in other directions.
And so, you know, what this looks like again is like their strategy, their objectives, the outcomes we're trying to build towards.
That kind of how do we craft a strategy that gets us there?
We start to break down like what are the different like topical opportunities here?
We develop kind of hypotheses, if you will, of like.
How do we start to get some momentum and build off of that?
We get signaled back and learn from that.
What's working, what's not?
Where do we double down?
Where do we cut?
And like that in itself, there's so much you can continue to build around at six plus months, several years.
time can just be on this.

so this idea of like first figure out the content, get more people to your website.
Then you can start to think about how do you distribute that in a variety of different ways.

And it's like, okay, we find that our best relationships start here.
Here's why.

so I can go through some of those again if helpful.

doesn't mean...

Yes, awesome.

Diversifying what we offer.
To the point you were bringing up of like, what are the deliverables look like and things of that nature.
It's not that we haven't done different things around this.
It's just that our pricing and packaging and like our approach isn't as clear for these other things.
So they tend to fall into this broader custom bucket.

But if you think about this from like an engagement manager standpoint, who's meeting these client relationships and maybe they've never done one of these things before, even though GrowthX has, and they're at a more, they have a more mature client and they're trying to figure out, how do they pitch this?
Like, what does it look like?
What's the deliverable?
They're feeling at times like, I'm not sure how to think about this, how to talk about it, how to price it.
And so, you know, we need to better arm them with that.
And like, what are the guardrails?
How would we approach this?
How would you even like build a strategy around that?
Like some of our engagement managers wouldn't even know where to start with if you ask them to build a programmatic SEO strategy, you know.
So we need to better arm them with that and train them and enable them around that.
So we're really at a phase where I think we're starting to feel this more acutely of like, how do you really diversify these work streams truly?
Rather than just always fall into these like, you know, flat renewals or even at times that can add churn risk, right?
If we don't have a clear answer for them of like how this can grow and progress and like what it looks like, that can frustrate the client too.
It's like, oh, sometimes they'll see, you know, Marcel may do like a thank you shared and they're like, I saw that thing Marcel shared.
It's so cool.
I love it.
Blah, blah, blah.
Like, why can't we do that?
So at times it's like kind of like, how do we enable the team to get there?
But also understand, is this something we want to take on is also been the complexity.
So we're, we're definitely at a point of friction with like really getting that, you know, better in motion, but that was part of the.
theme of like the offsite we were talking about last week of like, how do we actually start to learn from these things and get the team in a better place with that?
Perfect.
Cool.
What else could be helpful?
I know we only have five minutes left.
Let me see if, if I have some notes, but maybe from other things that I wanted to ask.
Perfect.

Cool.
Because I was, um, the only way in which of, I mean, I read them, I read material from the, this onboarding, uh, sort of like, um, uh, project.
But, um, many.

Concepts were still abstract, like how do we actually qualify a customer for complexity or how do we make them, how do we judge which is the best work stream, like how much time does it take for us, who does that, who says like no, these customers should start in the editorial or like at Google we would call that like the happy path, the happy path was one that like the standard, the one we know, the one we know is the one that will deliver us, maybe in a longer term, but to the best place possible.
And so all these decisions, I mean, who makes these decisions?
Yeah, no, good, good questions to be asking.
And yeah, I'd say like, you know, again, that's kind of where the strategy sprint is coming into play.
It's like trying to figure that out because previous to the strategy sprint, what we were trying to do was we had this benefit of a crazy fast sales cycle.
Like we were closing sometimes in like one or two calls.
And people were, you know, so excited to get started.
We wouldn't do enough validation and learning to like, what did they actually need?
And then they would come on with an assumption of what they need.
And then we would be like, this isn't actually, these stars aren't aligning here.
This isn't really making sense.
There's other reasons we introduced the strategy sprint, but that was one of the benefits of really trying to like, dig in there and try to prescribe what they need more clearly.

In practicality, though, I would say like, it is probably more rare where a customer comes in with some sort of, you know, they give us those objectives, we dig in and like, that we aren't going to go to some sort of editorial heavy component.
of what we do.
Anytime where we haven't left with some sort of heavy editorial thing, it's oftentimes because it comes in, in just like a unique way and a unique relationship.
It becomes more custom.
We already kind of knew we were building that.
We're using the strategy sprint to build that foundation.

But at least to my knowledge, there hasn't been a situation where like someone comes in and they're like, oh, you know, all of a sudden we thought we were going to do editorial, but we learned all these things and we're going to go do the super custom PSEO.
We're going to build this site for you or anything like that.
Oftentimes that comes later on.

don't know.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Onboard Andrea on customer use cases and engagement models.](https://fathom.video/share/uoUk7-bWzQsiCGRvseueeAsKFz8azk-J?tab=summary&timestamp=143.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Customer Need:** VC-backed companies hit a growth ceiling with paid search (demand capture) and need to diversify into organic channels (demand creation) for more sustainable, cost-effective growth.](https://fathom.video/share/uoUk7-bWzQsiCGRvseueeAsKFz8azk-J?tab=summary&timestamp=318.0)
  - [**Core Strategy:** Our "happy path" is starting with Editorial & Content Refresh. This builds a traffic foundation, generates rapid learning signals, and provides the data needed to effectively layer on other services like Programmatic SEO (PSEO).](https://fathom.video/share/uoUk7-bWzQsiCGRvseueeAsKFz8azk-J?tab=summary&timestamp=1080.0)
  - [**Ideal Customer Profile (ICP):** Clients must be bought-in on organic growth's long-term nature. They are not a fit if they expect overnight results or have highly regulated content that creates excessive review friction, which slows learning.](https://fathom.video/share/uoUk7-bWzQsiCGRvseueeAsKFz8azk-J?tab=summary&timestamp=496.0)
  - [**Growth Friction:** We need to productize advanced services (e.g., PSEO) with clear pricing and guardrails. This enables Engagement Managers to upsell and prevents churn risk from clients wanting services they see in other case studies.](https://fathom.video/share/uoUk7-bWzQsiCGRvseueeAsKFz8azk-J?tab=summary&timestamp=1313.0)

## Topics

### Customer Problem: Hitting the Paid Search Ceiling

  - [VC-backed companies often start with paid search (demand capture) for fast growth.](https://fathom.video/share/uoUk7-bWzQsiCGRvseueeAsKFz8azk-J?tab=summary&timestamp=318.0)
  - [**Problem:** This channel has a natural headroom limit, creating a growth ceiling and over-reliance on a single source of revenue.](https://fathom.video/share/uoUk7-bWzQsiCGRvseueeAsKFz8azk-J?tab=summary&timestamp=355.0)
  - [**Solution:** Diversify into organic channels (demand creation) like SEO and AEO, which offer:](https://fathom.video/share/uoUk7-bWzQsiCGRvseueeAsKFz8azk-J?tab=summary&timestamp=365.0)
      - [Higher headroom for long-term growth.](https://fathom.video/share/uoUk7-bWzQsiCGRvseueeAsKFz8azk-J?tab=summary&timestamp=365.0)
      - [More favorable economics.](https://fathom.video/share/uoUk7-bWzQsiCGRvseueeAsKFz8azk-J?tab=summary&timestamp=383.0)

### Our Solution: The "Happy Path" Engagement Model

  - [Our most successful engagements follow a specific sequence:](https://fathom.video/share/uoUk7-bWzQsiCGRvseueeAsKFz8azk-J?tab=summary&timestamp=1080.0)
    1.  **Editorial & Content Refresh (Foundation):**
          - [**Goal:** Build a traffic foundation and generate rapid learning signals.](https://fathom.video/share/uoUk7-bWzQsiCGRvseueeAsKFz8azk-J?tab=summary&timestamp=607.0)
          - [**Process:** Create high-quality, long-form content answering ICP questions.](https://fathom.video/share/uoUk7-bWzQsiCGRvseueeAsKFz8azk-J?tab=summary&timestamp=1105.0)
          - [**Outcome:** Compounding organic growth and rich data for future strategy.](https://fathom.video/share/uoUk7-bWzQsiCGRvseueeAsKFz8azk-J?tab=summary&timestamp=1133.0)
    2.  [**Distribution:** Layer on channels like newsletters *after* building a traffic base.](https://fathom.video/share/uoUk7-bWzQsiCGRvseueeAsKFz8azk-J?tab=summary&timestamp=1222.0)
    3.  [**Conversion:** Optimize the website to convert the new traffic.](https://fathom.video/share/uoUk7-bWzQsiCGRvseueeAsKFz8azk-J?tab=summary&timestamp=1222.0)
  - [**Rationale:** This sequence is critical. For example, a newsletter is ineffective without website traffic to drive sign-ups.](https://fathom.video/share/uoUk7-bWzQsiCGRvseueeAsKFz8azk-J?tab=summary&timestamp=1142.0)

### Ideal Customer Profile (ICP)

  - [**Fit:**](https://fathom.video/share/uoUk7-bWzQsiCGRvseueeAsKFz8azk-J?tab=summary&timestamp=496.0)
      - [**Buy-in:** Understands organic growth is a long-term investment.](https://fathom.video/share/uoUk7-bWzQsiCGRvseueeAsKFz8azk-J?tab=summary&timestamp=528.0)
      - [**Practicality:** Has realistic expectations for growth timelines.](https://fathom.video/share/uoUk7-bWzQsiCGRvseueeAsKFz8azk-J?tab=summary&timestamp=546.0)
  - [**Red Flags (High Friction):**](https://fathom.video/share/uoUk7-bWzQsiCGRvseueeAsKFz8azk-J?tab=summary&timestamp=546.0)
      - [**Unrealistic Expectations:** Demands overnight results.](https://fathom.video/share/uoUk7-bWzQsiCGRvseueeAsKFz8azk-J?tab=summary&timestamp=546.0)
      - [**Highly Regulated Content:** Requires extensive legal/medical review (e.g., Biologica).](https://fathom.video/share/uoUk7-bWzQsiCGRvseueeAsKFz8azk-J?tab=summary&timestamp=820.0)
          - [**Why it's a problem:** This friction slows content velocity, delaying learning signals and hindering our ability to prove value.](https://fathom.video/share/uoUk7-bWzQsiCGRvseueeAsKFz8azk-J?tab=summary&timestamp=593.0)
  - [**Resource:** George has a detailed ICP model; Andrea should connect with him.](https://fathom.video/share/uoUk7-bWzQsiCGRvseueeAsKFz8azk-J?tab=summary&timestamp=496.0)

### Operationalizing Complexity & Growth

  - [**Strategy Sprint:** An 8-week onboarding phase to align on goals, strategy, and content quality bars, especially for complex clients.](https://fathom.video/share/uoUk7-bWzQsiCGRvseueeAsKFz8azk-J?tab=summary&timestamp=727.0)
  - [**Expert Support:** Panzer is the internal expert for navigating highly complex, sticky client situations.](https://fathom.video/share/uoUk7-bWzQsiCGRvseueeAsKFz8azk-J?tab=summary&timestamp=964.0)
  - [**Growth Friction:** We need to productize advanced services (e.g., PSEO) to enable Engagement Managers to upsell and prevent churn risk.](https://fathom.video/share/uoUk7-bWzQsiCGRvseueeAsKFz8azk-J?tab=summary&timestamp=1313.0)
  - **Custom Deals:** The `#deal-desk` Slack channel is the official forum for scoping custom work and upsell opportunities.

## Next Steps

  - [**Andrea:** Connect with George to review the ICP model.](https://fathom.video/share/uoUk7-bWzQsiCGRvseueeAsKFz8azk-J?tab=summary&timestamp=496.0)
  - **Andrea:** Review the `#deal-desk` Slack channel for examples of custom deal scoping.

action_items.description: Schedule chat w/ George re: ICP + complexity model
